Do you want to start, Isabelle?
Sure.

So I think one important distinction is that we usually have customers come in because they either have problems with their loyalty program or with their promotion strategy, and if we think about those who are responsible for loyalty programs, it's usually that they are working with another vendor or an in-house solution that doesn't give them a ton of flexibility.
So that means that they often just have like a super basic earn and earn loyalty program that isn't really being adopted, and that just isn't really driving.
value for their customers, and then they're falling behind as a result.

So they're looking to solve that problem and work with a vendor like TalonOne, where there's a lot more flexibility in terms of the rewards that you can offer to your customers, also how it integrates with other parts of your tech stack, how it might solve omni-channel problems as well, where you get points online, but then you can't redeem them in store, so all of this complexity.
And then when it comes to the promotions, I'll call them promotions manager, but it's basically anyone who's running promotions.
The problem that they often have is, again, they are working with very rigid technology, which means that they can often only offer blanket discounts.
So they are kind of forced to offer 10% off discounts across a whole.
